title: CLAUDE.md
text: file provides guidance Claude Code (claude.ai/code) working code repository.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: socialchange R package decomposing social change components intraindividual change population turnover. package implements multiple decomposition methods analyzing aggregate-level outcomes change time due : - Intraindividual/within-unit changes (e.g., people changing attitudes) - Population turnover (cohort replacement, mortality, migration, coming--age) - Event-driven changes (e.g., countries joining/leaving EU)

title: CLAUDE.md
code: # Load package for development devtools::load_all()  # Build documentation (uses roxygen2) devtools::document()  # Check package devtools::check()  # Install package locally devtools::install()

title: CLAUDE.md
code: # Build pkgdown site pkgdown::build_site()

title: CLAUDE.md
text: See vignettes vignettes/ detailed examples: - vignettes/decompose_aggregated.qmd - Decomposition aggregated data - vignettes/simulate.qmd - Simulation examples

title: CLAUDE.md
text: package implements three main decomposition approaches: decompose_events(): Decomposes change units enter/exit population discrete time points Works two datasets: events data (entry/exit dates) outcomes data (values time point) Formula interface: Outcome ~ Unit + Time Separates change “change” (within-unit) “replacement” (turnover) components Computes counterfactuals showing happen change replacement decompose_aggregated(): data aggregated age covariates Uses microsimulation approach randomly order demographic events Requires: stacked panel data age, period, cell counts Requires: fun_y function computes outcome data Decomposes : intraindividual change, coming--age, mortality, (optionally) migration sim_social_change(): Forward simulation social change dynamics User provides functions : outcome (fun_y), mortality, coming--age, migration, state transitions Returns detailed event--event records showing exact contribution component

title: CLAUDE.md
text: CR-IC decomposition (R/cr_ic.R): Classical cohort replacement vs. intraindividual change decomposition using algebraic, linear, model-based approaches APC models (R/apc.R): Age-Period-Cohort models using orthogonal polynomial contrasts handle identification issues

title: CLAUDE.md
text: three main approaches use data.table extensively: - Input data data frames data.tables - Formula interfaces follow R conventions: Outcome ~ Unit + Time - Functions internally rename columns standardized names (unit, index, outcome, etc.) - Time/period columns coerced numeric calculations - functions return S3 objects class attributes custom print/plot methods

title: CLAUDE.md
text: main functions return list objects custom S3 classes: - decompose_events → class decompose_events - decompose_aggregated → class social_change_decomp - sim_social_change → class social_change_sim - cr_ic → class cr_ic_decomposition - apc → class apc_model custom print() often plot() methods defined.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Key dependencies: - data.table: data manipulation (imported) - ggplot2: Plotting (imported) - checkmate: Input validation - weightedcontrasts: Custom package APC models (GitHub: elbersb/weightedcontrasts)

title: CLAUDE.md
text: exported functions use roxygen2 documentation Documentation files functions roxygen2 version 8.0.0 (see DESCRIPTION) Use @import data.table @import ggplot2 directives Use @export exported functions S3 methods exported @export registered S3method() NAMESPACE

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Located data/: - gssrac.rda: General Social Survey data racial attitudes - eu_membership.rda: EU country entry/exit dates - wpp_data.rda: UN World Population Prospects data (1950+) Dataset documentation R/data.R man/*.Rd files. Data preparation scripts data-raw/.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Funcationality implemented yet.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: decompose_aggregated() properly handle within-cell transitions (e.g., smokers becoming non-smokers). function decomposes change : - Intraindividual change - Mortality - Coming--age - Migration (optional) input data includes transitions cells, transition effects separately identified instead get absorbed intraindividual change component. Additionally: Mortality coming--age estimated simple population differences periods: mortality = n1 - n2 coming_of_age = n2 - n1 transitions occur, differences longer accurately reflect demographic events code uses pmax(0, ...) prevent negative mortality/coming--age counts, masks underlying problem Cells net inflow transitions show 0 mortality even deaths occurred Workaround: fundamental limitation aggregated decomposition approach. simulation function sim_social_change() properly tracks transitions separate component, decompose_aggregated() recover already-aggregated data. practice: Use decompose_aggregated() data without significant within-cell transitions, aware transition effects misattributed intraindividual change.

title: Decomposing social change on aggregated data
text: now feed simulated data event-based decomposition: event-based decomposition exactly recovered results.
code: library("data.table")  data <- data.table(     age = c(20:39),     n = c(rep(100, 10), seq.int(100, 10, by = -10)) )  support_for_pensions <- function(data, time) {     data[, (age - 20) * 1 / 20] } coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = 20, n = 100) } mortality <- function(data, period) {     data[, ifelse(age >= 30, 10, 0)] }  library("socialchange") simresult <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = support_for_pensions,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(simresult, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component   Value #>  At initial                0.3758 #>  At end                    0.3758 #>  Total change              0.0000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.2500 #>  - Population turnover    -0.2500 #>    - Mortality            -0.1207 #>    - Out-migration         0.0000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.1293 #>    - In-migration          0.0000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(simresult$snapshot) # we know that y depends only on age here model <- lm(y ~ age, data = stacked_data) predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model, newdata = newdata) }  decompresult <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y) print(decompresult, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component   Value #>  At initial (modeled)      0.3758 #>  At end (modeled)          0.3758 #>  Total change              0.0000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.2500 #>  - Population turnover    -0.2500 #>    - Mortality            -0.1207 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.1293 #> Assumes no in- or out-migration.

title: Decomposing social change on aggregated data
text: event-based decomposition, first try misspecified model ignores gender entirely. Still, decomposition case recovers result almost exactly: results fully-specified model:
code: data <- data.table(     age = c(20:39, 20:39),     gender = c(rep("f", 20), rep("m", 20)),     n = c(         c(rep(50, 10), seq.int(50, 5, by = -5)),         c(rep(50, 10), seq.int(50, 5, by = -5))     ) )  coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = c(20, 20), gender = c("f", "m"), n = c(50, 50)) } mortality <- function(data, period) {     data[, ifelse(age >= 30, 5, 0)] } support_for_pensions <- function(data, time) {     data[, ifelse(gender == "f", 0.3758, (age - 20) * 1 / 20)] }  simresult <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = support_for_pensions,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(simresult, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value #>  At initial                0.37580 #>  At end                    0.37580 #>  Total change              0.00000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.12533 #>  - Population turnover    -0.12533 #>    - Mortality            -0.06051 #>    - Out-migration         0.00000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.06482 #>    - In-migration          0.00000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(simresult$snapshot) model_no_gender <- lm(y ~ age + period, data = stacked_data) cor(stacked_data$y, predict(model_no_gender)) #> [1] 0.6870648 predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model_no_gender, newdata = newdata) }  decomp <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y) print(decomp, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value #>  At initial (modeled)      0.37580 #>  At end (modeled)          0.37580 #>  Total change              0.00000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.12500 #>  - Population turnover    -0.12500 #>    - Mortality            -0.06036 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.06464 #> Assumes no in- or out-migration. model_gender <- lm(y ~ age * gender, data = stacked_data) cor(stacked_data$y, predict(model_gender)) #> [1] 1 predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model_gender, newdata = newdata) }  decomp <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y, "gender") print(decomp, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value #>  At initial (modeled)      0.37580 #>  At end (modeled)          0.37580 #>  Total change              0.00000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.12488 #>  - Population turnover    -0.12488 #>    - Mortality            -0.06031 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.06457 #> Assumes no in- or out-migration.

title: Decomposing social change on aggregated data
text: , first try misspecified model ignores gender entirely. , decomposition recovers result almost exactly despite misspecification: results fully-specified model:
code: ic_only <- function(data, time) {     data[, ifelse(gender == "f", 0.2, 0.4) + time / 10] }  simresult <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = ic_only,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(simresult, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component     Value #>  At initial                0.300000 #>  At end                    0.800000 #>  Total change              0.500000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.500000 #>  - Population turnover     0.000000 #>    - Mortality             0.000011 #>    - Out-migration         0.000000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.000011 #>    - In-migration          0.000000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(simresult$snapshot) model_no_gender <- lm(y ~ age + period, data = stacked_data) cor(stacked_data$y, predict(model_no_gender)) #> [1] 0.8629489 predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model_no_gender, newdata = newdata) }  decomp <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y) print(decomp, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component Value #>  At initial (modeled)       0.3 #>  At end (modeled)           0.8 #>  Total change               0.5 #>  - Intraindividual change   0.5 #>  - Population turnover      0.0 #>    - Mortality              0.0 #>    - Coming-of-age          0.0 #> Assumes no in- or out-migration. model_gender <- lm(y ~ age + period + gender, data = stacked_data) cor(stacked_data$y, predict(model_gender)) #> [1] 1 predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model_gender, newdata = newdata) }  decomp <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y, "gender") print(decomp, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component     Value #>  At initial (modeled)      0.300000 #>  At end (modeled)          0.800000 #>  Total change              0.500000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.500000 #>  - Population turnover     0.000000 #>    - Mortality            -0.000077 #>    - Coming-of-age         0.000077 #> Assumes no in- or out-migration.

title: Decomposing social change on aggregated data
text: , first try misspecified model ignores gender entirely. , decomposition recovers result almost exactly despite misspecification: results fully-specified model:
code: pt_only <- function(data, time) {     data[, ifelse(gender == "f", 0.2, 0.4) + 0.7516 + (time - age + 20) / 10] }  simresult <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = pt_only,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(simresult, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component  Value #>  At initial               0.3000 #>  At end                   0.8000 #>  Total change             0.5000 #>  - Intraindividual change 0.0000 #>  - Population turnover    0.5000 #>    - Mortality            0.2415 #>    - Out-migration        0.0000 #>    - Coming-of-age        0.2585 #>    - In-migration         0.0000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(simresult$snapshot) model_no_gender <- lm(y ~ age + period, data = stacked_data) cor(stacked_data$y, predict(model_no_gender)) #> [1] 0.9864554 predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model_no_gender, newdata = newdata) }  decomp <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y) print(decomp, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component  Value #>  At initial (modeled)     0.3000 #>  At end (modeled)         0.8000 #>  Total change             0.5000 #>  - Intraindividual change 0.0000 #>  - Population turnover    0.5000 #>    - Mortality            0.2415 #>    - Coming-of-age        0.2585 #> Assumes no in- or out-migration. model_gender <- lm(y ~ age + period + gender, data = stacked_data) cor(stacked_data$y, predict(model_gender)) #> [1] 1 predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model_gender, newdata = newdata) }  decomp <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y, "gender") print(decomp, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component  Value #>  At initial (modeled)     0.3000 #>  At end (modeled)         0.8000 #>  Total change             0.5000 #>  - Intraindividual change 0.0000 #>  - Population turnover    0.5000 #>    - Mortality            0.2412 #>    - Coming-of-age        0.2588 #> Assumes no in- or out-migration.

title: Decomposing social change on aggregated data
text: Compare decomposition: Compare decomposition: Compare decomposition:
code: data <- data.table(     age = c(20:39, 20:39),     smoking = c(rep("smoker", 20), rep("nonsmoker", 20)),     n = c(         c(rep(90, 10), seq.int(90, 0, by = -12), 0, 0),         c(rep(10, 10), seq.int(10, 1, by = -1))     ) ) coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = c(20, 20), smoking = c("smoker", "nonsmoker"), n = c(90, 10)) } mortality <- function(data, period) {     data[, fcase(         # we need to make sure that we still have enough individuals, therefore the pmin         age >= 30 & smoking == "smoker", pmin(12, n),         age >= 30 & smoking == "nonsmoker", 1,         default = 0     )] } smoking_in_public <- function(data, time) {     data[, ifelse(smoking == "smoker", 0.9, 0.6)] } smoking1 <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = smoking_in_public,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(smoking1, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component     Value #>  At initial                0.867686 #>  At end                    0.867686 #>  Total change              0.000000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.000000 #>  - Population turnover     0.000000 #>    - Mortality            -0.000803 #>    - Out-migration         0.000000 #>    - Coming-of-age         0.000803 #>    - In-migration          0.000000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(smoking1$snapshot) model <- lm(y ~ age + period + smoking, data = stacked_data) cor(stacked_data$y, predict(model)) #> [1] 1 predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model, newdata = newdata) }  decomp <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y, "smoking") print(decomp, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component     Value #>  At initial (modeled)      0.867686 #>  At end (modeled)          0.867686 #>  Total change              0.000000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.000000 #>  - Population turnover     0.000000 #>    - Mortality            -0.000903 #>    - Coming-of-age         0.000903 #> Assumes no in- or out-migration. smoking_in_public <- function(data, time) {     data[, ifelse(smoking == "smoker", 0.9 - time*0.05, 0.6 - time*0.1)] }  smoking2 <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = smoking_in_public,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(smoking2, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component     Value #>  At initial                0.867686 #>  At end                    0.590757 #>  Total change             -0.276929 #>  - Intraindividual change -0.276882 #>  - Population turnover    -0.000047 #>    - Mortality            -0.001092 #>    - Out-migration         0.000000 #>    - Coming-of-age         0.001045 #>    - In-migration          0.000000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(smoking2$snapshot) model <- lm(y ~ age + period * smoking, data = stacked_data) cor(stacked_data$y, predict(model)) #> [1] 1 predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model, newdata = newdata) }  decomp <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y, "smoking") print(decomp, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component     Value #>  At initial (modeled)      0.867686 #>  At end (modeled)          0.590757 #>  Total change             -0.276929 #>  - Intraindividual change -0.277101 #>  - Population turnover     0.000173 #>    - Mortality            -0.001195 #>    - Coming-of-age         0.001368 #> Assumes no in- or out-migration. coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = c(20, 20), smoking = c("smoker", "nonsmoker"), n = c(90 - period * 15, 10 + period * 15)) }  smoking3 <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = smoking_in_public,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(smoking3, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value #>  At initial                0.86769 #>  At end                    0.50476 #>  Total change             -0.36293 #>  - Intraindividual change -0.29130 #>  - Population turnover    -0.07162 #>    - Mortality            -0.01067 #>    - Out-migration         0.00000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.06095 #>    - In-migration          0.00000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(smoking3$snapshot) model <- lm(y ~ age + period * smoking, data = stacked_data) cor(stacked_data$y, predict(model)) #> [1] 1 predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model, newdata = newdata) }  decomp <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y, "smoking") print(decomp, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value #>  At initial (modeled)      0.86769 #>  At end (modeled)          0.50476 #>  Total change             -0.36293 #>  - Intraindividual change -0.29133 #>  - Population turnover    -0.07159 #>    - Mortality            -0.01077 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.06082 #> Assumes no in- or out-migration.

title: Decomposing social change on aggregated data
text: Compare decomposition:
code: data <- data.table(     age = c(20:39, 20:39),     smoking = c(rep("smoker", 20), rep("nonsmoker", 20)),     n = c(         c(rep(90, 10), seq.int(90, 0, by = -12), 0, 0),         c(rep(10, 10), seq.int(10, 1, by = -1))     ) ) coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = c(20, 20), smoking = c("smoker", "nonsmoker"), n = c(90, 10)) } mortality <- function(data, period) {     data[, fcase(         # we need to make sure that we still have enough individuals, therefore the pmin         age >= 30 & smoking == "smoker", pmin(12, n),         age >= 30 & smoking == "nonsmoker", 1,         default = 0     )] } smoking_in_public <- function(data, time) {     data[, ifelse(smoking == "smoker", 0.9, 0.6)] } smoking1 <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = smoking_in_public,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(smoking1, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component     Value #>  At initial                0.867686 #>  At end                    0.867686 #>  Total change              0.000000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.000000 #>  - Population turnover     0.000000 #>    - Mortality            -0.000803 #>    - Out-migration         0.000000 #>    - Coming-of-age         0.000803 #>    - In-migration          0.000000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(smoking1$snapshot) model <- lm(y ~ age + period + smoking, data = stacked_data) cor(stacked_data$y, predict(model)) #> [1] 1 predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model, newdata = newdata) }  decomp <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y, "smoking") print(decomp, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component     Value #>  At initial (modeled)      0.867686 #>  At end (modeled)          0.867686 #>  Total change              0.000000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.000000 #>  - Population turnover     0.000000 #>    - Mortality            -0.000903 #>    - Coming-of-age         0.000903 #> Assumes no in- or out-migration.

text: Compare decomposition:
code: smoking_in_public <- function(data, time) {     data[, ifelse(smoking == "smoker", 0.9 - time*0.05, 0.6 - time*0.1)] }  smoking2 <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = smoking_in_public,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(smoking2, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component     Value #>  At initial                0.867686 #>  At end                    0.590757 #>  Total change             -0.276929 #>  - Intraindividual change -0.276882 #>  - Population turnover    -0.000047 #>    - Mortality            -0.001092 #>    - Out-migration         0.000000 #>    - Coming-of-age         0.001045 #>    - In-migration          0.000000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(smoking2$snapshot) model <- lm(y ~ age + period * smoking, data = stacked_data) cor(stacked_data$y, predict(model)) #> [1] 1 predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model, newdata = newdata) }  decomp <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y, "smoking") print(decomp, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component     Value #>  At initial (modeled)      0.867686 #>  At end (modeled)          0.590757 #>  Total change             -0.276929 #>  - Intraindividual change -0.277101 #>  - Population turnover     0.000173 #>    - Mortality            -0.001195 #>    - Coming-of-age         0.001368 #> Assumes no in- or out-migration.

title: Decomposing social change on aggregated data
text: Compare decomposition:
code: coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = c(20, 20), smoking = c("smoker", "nonsmoker"), n = c(90 - period * 15, 10 + period * 15)) }  smoking3 <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = smoking_in_public,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(smoking3, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value #>  At initial                0.86769 #>  At end                    0.50476 #>  Total change             -0.36293 #>  - Intraindividual change -0.29130 #>  - Population turnover    -0.07162 #>    - Mortality            -0.01067 #>    - Out-migration         0.00000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.06095 #>    - In-migration          0.00000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(smoking3$snapshot) model <- lm(y ~ age + period * smoking, data = stacked_data) cor(stacked_data$y, predict(model)) #> [1] 1 predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model, newdata = newdata) }  decomp <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y, "smoking") print(decomp, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value #>  At initial (modeled)      0.86769 #>  At end (modeled)          0.50476 #>  Total change             -0.36293 #>  - Intraindividual change -0.29133 #>  - Population turnover    -0.07159 #>    - Mortality            -0.01077 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.06082 #> Assumes no in- or out-migration.

title: Simulating social change
text: Building simulation requires three steps: Define population Define outcome function variable interest Define population dynamics (coming age, mortality, - -migration) Step 1: first step define initial population. example, define simple population aged 20 39, uniform distribution age 30, declining age 39. dataset define needs least two columns: age n. can add columns want describe complicated populations, ’ll stick simple setup now. ’ll call row dataset population cell. extreme case, can also define population individuals n just 1. ’s simple population: Step 2: Next, define variable interest want simulate. , define function takes arguments population cells time continuous variable. Time really needs continuous - need know outcome every individual population terms discrete ‘years’ (whatever time scale), every point -well. example, use outcome dependent age: age 20, outcome 0, age 40 outcome 1, values linearly increasing age. purely function age, time, real-world survey, outcome “support investing longevity research” “support increasing pensions” - ’ll just use latter example: defined population terms one variable (age), members age also identical outcome value. Step 3: next step define population dynamics. requires defining four functions: fun_mortality(data, period) - needs return vector counts population cell fun_outmigration(data, period) - needs return vector counts population cell fun_coming_of_age(data, period) - needs return data frame new population cells column n fun_inmigration(data, period) - needs return data frame new population cells column n functions defined period--period. practice, often years. instance, mortality function return counts define many members population die within next period, population cell. -migration function defined way, just number people migrating population cell. coming age function -migration functions different: return data frame describes new population members added period--period. functions flexible enough describe complicated real-world population dynamics. first simple example, define functions way keeps population stable: don’t migration, mortality coming--age balances perfectly - every period 100 new members age 20 added, 100 members age 30 die. four functions optional, just define coming--age mortality function: ’re now ready start simulation: simulation result displays overview period, well summary total decomposition change. results year show indeed population stable: end every period population 1550, stable mean 0.38 - .e, 38% population supports increasing pensions. However, stability bit deceptive, everyone’s opinion actually keeps constantly changing. can see looking “Intraindividual change” component, +0.25. Roughly speaking, without population turnover, expected mean support increasing pensions increased quite bit, 63%. makes sense, ’ve designed simulation way support increases age. turn, younger people much less likely support increasing pensions. Hence, enter population, effect decreasing mean. true mortality. Hence, population turnover component negative. simulation, two components balance exactly, leading expected complete stabilty year--year mean. slightly complex setup, can add covariates simulation. instance, can introduce gender covariate. example, split former population two equally-distributed genders: also adjust population dynamics functions keep population stable. Note don’t depend gender (): reran simulation settings, get exact results . introduce slight variation, though, determine one gender actually fixed opinion support increasing pensions. gender behaves : Although overall mean doesn’t change time, decomposition results changed. men now contribute population turnover intraindividual change (women ), components half . previous scenario, overall outcome stable, intraindividual change well population turnover, cancelling . can also construct case shows intraindividual change, making outcome function dependent time (possibly covariates, ), age. expected, results show outcome increased 0.1 year--year, change due intraindividual change: Conversely, can also construct scenario change explained population turnover - older cohorts replaced younger cohorts. , define outcome function individual’s birth cohort: constants chosen two cases align. important thing outcome depends birth cohort (.e. cohort = year - age). results show exact dynamic – initial mean 0.3 increase 0.1 every year –, different explanations: Now change completely explained population turnover. one ever changes mind, social change occurs older cohorts low outcome values die younger cohorts higher outcome values follow. Hence, mortality coming--age contribute equally total change.
code: library("data.table")  data <- data.table(     age = c(20:39),     n = c(rep(100, 10), seq.int(100, 10, by = -10)) )  data #>       age     n #>     <int> <num> #>  1:    20   100 #>  2:    21   100 #>  3:    22   100 #>  4:    23   100 #>  5:    24   100 #>  6:    25   100 #>  7:    26   100 #>  8:    27   100 #>  9:    28   100 #> 10:    29   100 #> 11:    30   100 #> 12:    31    90 #> 13:    32    80 #> 14:    33    70 #> 15:    34    60 #> 16:    35    50 #> 17:    36    40 #> 18:    37    30 #> 19:    38    20 #> 20:    39    10 #>       age     n #>     <int> <num> support_for_pensions <- function(data, time) {     data[, (age - 20) * 1 / 20] } # e.g., at age 20,   support_for_pensions is 0 #       at age 20.5, support_for_pensions is 0.026 #       at age 40,   support_for_pensions is 1 coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = 20, n = 100) } mortality <- function(data, period) {     data[, ifelse(age >= 30, 10, 0)] } library(socialchange)  simresult <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = support_for_pensions,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) simresult #> Overview by period: #>  period   mean    N intraindividual coming_of_age mortality inmigration #>       0 0.3758 1550              NA            NA        NA          NA #>       1 0.3758 1550            0.05      -0.02586  -0.02414           0 #>       2 0.3758 1550            0.05      -0.02585  -0.02415           0 #>       3 0.3758 1550            0.05      -0.02583  -0.02417           0 #>       4 0.3758 1550            0.05      -0.02586  -0.02414           0 #>       5 0.3758 1550            0.05      -0.02587  -0.02413           0 #>  outmigration #>            NA #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>  #> Decomposition of total change: #>                 Component   Value #>  At initial                0.3758 #>  At end                    0.3758 #>  Total change              0.0000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.2500 #>  - Population turnover    -0.2500 #>    - Mortality            -0.1207 #>    - Out-migration         0.0000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.1293 #>    - In-migration          0.0000 data <- data.table(     age = c(20:39, 20:39),     gender = c(rep("f", 20), rep("m", 20)),     n = c(         c(rep(50, 10), seq.int(50, 5, by = -5)),         c(rep(50, 10), seq.int(50, 5, by = -5))     ) ) coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = c(20, 20), gender = c("f", "m"), n = c(50, 50)) } mortality <- function(data, period) {     data[, ifelse(age >= 30, 5, 0)] } support_for_pensions <- function(data, time) {     data[, ifelse(gender == "f", 0.3758, (age - 20) * 1 / 20)] } simresult <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = support_for_pensions,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) simresult #> Overview by period: #>  period   mean    N intraindividual coming_of_age mortality inmigration #>       0 0.3758 1550              NA            NA        NA          NA #>       1 0.3758 1550         0.02506      -0.01297  -0.01209           0 #>       2 0.3758 1550         0.02494      -0.01288  -0.01206           0 #>       3 0.3758 1550         0.02483      -0.01285  -0.01198           0 #>       4 0.3758 1550         0.02497      -0.01293  -0.01204           0 #>       5 0.3758 1550         0.02502      -0.01294  -0.01208           0 #>  outmigration #>            NA #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>  #> Decomposition of total change: #>                 Component    Value #>  At initial                0.37580 #>  At end                    0.37580 #>  Total change              0.00000 #>  - title: Simulating social change
text: slightly complex setup, can add covariates simulation. instance, can introduce gender covariate. example, split former population two equally-distributed genders: also adjust population dynamics functions keep population stable. Note don’t depend gender (): reran simulation settings, get exact results . introduce slight variation, though, determine one gender actually fixed opinion support increasing pensions. gender behaves : Although overall mean doesn’t change time, decomposition results changed. men now contribute population turnover intraindividual change (women ), components half .
code: data <- data.table(     age = c(20:39, 20:39),     gender = c(rep("f", 20), rep("m", 20)),     n = c(         c(rep(50, 10), seq.int(50, 5, by = -5)),         c(rep(50, 10), seq.int(50, 5, by = -5))     ) ) coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = c(20, 20), gender = c("f", "m"), n = c(50, 50)) } mortality <- function(data, period) {     data[, ifelse(age >= 30, 5, 0)] } support_for_pensions <- function(data, time) {     data[, ifelse(gender == "f", 0.3758, (age - 20) * 1 / 20)] } simresult <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = support_for_pensions,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) simresult #> Overview by period: #>  period   mean    N intraindividual coming_of_age mortality inmigration #>       0 0.3758 1550              NA            NA        NA          NA #>       1 0.3758 1550         0.02506      -0.01297  -0.01209           0 #>       2 0.3758 1550         0.02494      -0.01288  -0.01206           0 #>       3 0.3758 1550         0.02483      -0.01285  -0.01198           0 #>       4 0.3758 1550         0.02497      -0.01293  -0.01204           0 #>       5 0.3758 1550         0.02502      -0.01294  -0.01208           0 #>  outmigration #>            NA #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>  #> Decomposition of total change: #>                 Component    Value #>  At initial                0.37580 #>  At end                    0.37580 #>  Total change              0.00000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.12483 #>  - Population turnover    -0.12483 #>    - Mortality            -0.06025 #>    - Out-migration         0.00000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.06458 #>    - In-migration          0.00000

what: Adding state changes: Smoking and differential mortality
title: Simulating social change
text: second example, try simulate somewhat realistic scenario attitudes towards smoking changed Western societies. start simple build complex simulations piece piece. outcome, consider survey question “smoking public allowed?”. start similar population , time split smokers non-smokers, vast majority population smokes (90% younger age groups). construct initial population already way shows differential mortality: Smokers die early, meaning higher ages, proportion non-smokers increases: disregard migration, create coming--age mortality function reproduces current population: Lastly, define static outcome differs smokers non-smokers, otherwise stable: 90% smokers agree smoking public allowed, 60% non-smokers : Running simulation scenario shows static population: neither intra-individual change net effect population turnover, although mortality decreases mean (smokers die earlier, drawing mean), coming--age brings back . two forces therefore perfectly cancel . slightly realistic scenario, assume smokers non-smokers change mind smoking public, however, smokers change mind half rate compared non-smokers: expected, population turnover component still effectively zero, showing dynamics . However, now substantial intraindividual change, means outcome decreases 87% 59% - due intraindividual change. attitudes towards smoking changing, likely also reflected individual behavior. means fewer people may pick smoking first place. can model altering coming--age function: ’re still adding 100 members every year population, every year proportion smokers declines: three quarters first year 15% last year. Given now non-smokers coming age, see now bigger contribution population turnover component, driven coming--age. final mechanism change behavior existing smokers: every year, give smoking, likely also influence opinion smoking public. simplicity, assume former smokers start behave exactly non-smokers moment stop smoking. also keep transitions logic quite simple - assume random set 15% smokers become non-smokers every period. make complicated, instance, also stratifying transitions age. now include transition logic simulation: now see bigger contribution intraindividual change people become nonsmokers start share group’s opinion smoking public. side effect new logic population longer stable: number people population increasing, mean age. now also people grow older 39, mortality function setup didn’t account people attaining older ages: side effect fewer people smoking, decreases chance mortality. complicated simulation might introduce “former smoker” group retains mortality disadvantage smokers old age. Graphically, can show proportion smokers age group follows:
code: data <- data.table(     age = c(20:39, 20:39),     smoking = c(rep("smoker", 20), rep("nonsmoker", 20)),     n = c(         c(rep(90, 10), seq.int(90, 0, by = -12), 0, 0),         c(rep(10, 10), seq.int(10, 1, by = -1))     ) ) # reshape the data to show proportion non-smoking dcast(data, age ~ smoking, value.var = "n")[,     .(age, nonsmoker, smoker, prop_non_smoking = nonsmoker / (smoker + nonsmoker)) ] #> Key: <age> #>       age nonsmoker smoker prop_non_smoking #>     <int>     <num>  <num>            <num> #>  1:    20        10     90        0.1000000 #>  2:    21        10     90        0.1000000 #>  3:    22        10     90        0.1000000 #>  4:    23        10     90        0.1000000 #>  5:    24        10     90        0.1000000 #>  6:    25        10     90        0.1000000 #>  7:    26        10     90        0.1000000 #>  8:    27        10     90        0.1000000 #>  9:    28        10     90        0.1000000 #> 10:    29        10     90        0.1000000 #> 11:    30        10     90        0.1000000 #> 12:    31         9     78        0.1034483 #> 13:    32         8     66        0.1081081 #> 14:    33         7     54        0.1147541 #> 15:    34         6     42        0.1250000 #> 16:    35         5     30        0.1428571 #> 17:    36         4     18        0.1818182 #> 18:    37         3      6        0.3333333 #> 19:    38         2      0        1.0000000 #> 20:    39         1      0        1.0000000 #>       age nonsmoker smoker prop_non_smoking #>     <int>     <num>  <num>            <num> coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = c(20, 20), smoking = c("smoker", "nonsmoker"), n = c(90, 10)) } mortality <- function(data, period) {     data[, fcase(         # we need to make sure that we still have enough individuals, therefore the pmin         age >= 30 & smoking == "smoker", pmin(12, n),         age >= 30 & smoking == "nonsmoker", 1,         default = 0     )] } smoking_in_public <- function(data, time) {     data[, ifelse(smoking == "smoker", 0.9, 0.6)] } smoking1 <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = smoking_in_public,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) smoking1 #> Overview by period: #>  period   mean    N intraindividual coming_of_age  mortality inmigration #>       0 0.8677 1439              NA            NA         NA          NA #>       1 0.8677 1439               0     0.0001327 -0.0001327           0 #>       2 0.8677 1439               0     0.0001483 -0.0001483           0 #>       3 0.8677 1439               0     0.0001511 -0.0001511           0 #>       4 0.8677 1439               0     0.0001482 -0.0001482           0 #>       5 0.8677 1439               0     0.0001670 -0.0001670           0 #>  outmigration #>            NA #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>  #> Decomposition of total change: #>                 Component     Value #>  At initial                0.867686 #>  At end                    0.867686 #>  Total change              0.000000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.000000 #>  - Population turnover     0.000000 #>    - Mortality            -0.000747 #>    - Out-migration         0.000000 #>    - Coming-of-age         0.000747 #>    - In-migration          0.000000 smoking_in_public <- function(data, time) {     data[, ifelse(smoking == "smoker", 0.9 - time*0.05, 0.6 - time*0.1)] }  smoking2 <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = smoking_in_public,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) smoking2 #> Overview by period: #>  period   mean    N intraindividual coming_of_age  mortality inmigration #>       0 0.8677 1439 0.00025033 -0.0002412           0 #>  outmigration #>            NA #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>  #> Decomposition of total change: #> Population turnover    -0.000134 #>    - Mortality            -0.001124 #>    - Out-migration         0.000000 #>    - Coming-of-age         0.000990 #>    - In-migration          0.000000 coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = c(20, 20), smoking = c("smoker", "nonsmoker"), n = c(90 - period * 15, 10 + period * 15)) } smoking3 <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = smoking_in_public,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) smoking3 #> Overview by period: #>  period   mean    N intraindividual coming_of_age  mortality inmigration #>       0 0.8677 1439              NA            NA         NA          NA #>       1 0.8087 1439        -0.05558     -0.003135 -0.0003227 -0.016955 -0.0028972           0 #>       5 0.5048 1439        -0.06198     -0.022116 -0.0051659           0 #>  outmigration #>            NA #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #>        -0.36293 #>  - Intraindividual change -0.29130 #>  - Population turnover    -0.07162 #>    - Mortality            -0.01078 #>    - Out-migration         0.00000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.06084 #>    - In-migration          0.00000 transitions <- function(data, time) {     n_smokers <- data[smoking == "smoker", sum(n)]     # column names need to map to data - the to_ prefix indicates the transition     data.table(smoking = "smoker", to_smoking = "nonsmoker", n = round(n_smokers * 0.15)) } smoking4 <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = smoking_in_public,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality,     fun_transitions = transitions ) smoking4 #> Overview by period: #>  period   mean    N intraindividual coming_of_age mortality inmigration #>       0 0.8677 1439              NA            NA        NA          NA #>       1 0.7617 1439         -0.1026     -0.001349 -0.001996 -0.004521 -0.012034           0 #>       5 0.2582 1488         -0.1132     -0.006684 -0.015239           0 #>  outmigration #>            NA #>             0 #>             0 #>             0 #> -0.01762 #>    - In-migration          0.00000 by_year <- rbindlist(smoking4$snapshot) by_year[, .(mean_age = weighted.mean(age, n), max_age = max(age)), by = .(period)] #>    period mean_age max_age #>     <num>    <num>   <num> #> 1:      0 26.87630      39 #> 2:      1 26.87630      39 #> 3:      2 26.92042      39 #> 4:      3 26.98417      39 #> 5:      4 27.10688      40 #> 6:      5 27.25856      41 library(ggplot2)  p_smoker <- by_year[, .(p_smoker = weighted.mean(smoking == "smoker", n)), by = .(age, period)] ggplot(p_smoker, aes(x = period, y = age, fill = p_smoker)) + geom_tile() + theme_bw()

title: What the Package Does (Title Case)
text: basic example, look decomposition EU membership, whether population growth EU due intracountry changes population, due admission new countries. decomposition unit country. run decompose_events decomposition function, need two datasets: One dataset contains events, case dates countries entered exited EU. dataset included socialchange package: second dataset contains outcome want decompose point time change events, plus start end period interest. EU formed 1952, countries entered exited 1973, 1981, 1986, 1995, 2004, 2007, 2013, 2020. Countries, course, entered specific dates, population data provided yearly resolutions, assume error introduced using yearly resolution small. socialchange package also includes total population data countries world 1950 onwards, also includes required data: run decompose_events function, need ensure columns unit time parameters named , prepare data follows: Finally, run decomposition. specify two datasets, formula form Outcome ~ Unit + Time. also need specify end data, aggregation function. Often mean, case needs sum: outcome decomposition tells us intracountry changes population account 30% total population increase (80 million), additions one (Br)exit account 70% total population increase (184 million). also possible produce figure shows decomposition:
code: library(socialchange) socialchange::eu_membership #>           country       date event_type #> 1         Belgium 1952-07-23    initial #> 2          France 1952-07-23    initial #> 3         Germany 1952-07-23    initial #> 4           Italy 1952-07-23    initial #> 5      Luxembourg 1952-07-23    initial #> 6     Netherlands 1952-07-23    initial #> 7  United Kingdom 1973-01-01      entry #> 8         Denmark 1973-01-01      entry #> 9         Ireland 1973-01-01      entry #> 10         Greece 1981-01-01      entry #> 11       Portugal 1986-01-01      entry #> 12          Spain 1986-01-01      entry #> 13        Austria 1995-01-01      entry #> 14         Sweden 1995-01-01      entry #> 15        Finland 1995-01-01      entry #> 16         Cyprus 2004-05-01      entry #> 17          Malta 2004-05-01      entry #> 18        Hungary 2004-05-01      entry #> 19         Poland 2004-05-01      entry #> 20       Slovakia 2004-05-01      entry #> 21         Latvia 2004-05-01      entry #> 22        Estonia 2004-05-01      entry #> 23      Lithuania 2004-05-01      entry #> 24        Czechia 2004-05-01      entry #> 25       Slovenia 2004-05-01      entry #> 26       Bulgaria 2007-01-01      entry #> 27        Romania 2007-01-01      entry #> 28        Croatia 2013-07-01      entry #> 29 United Kingdom 2020-01-31       exit library(data.table) #>  #> Attaching package: 'data.table' #> The following object is masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     %notin% wpp_data <- as.data.table(socialchange::wpp_data) # population data is in 1,000 wpp_data[Location == "Belgium" & Time %in% c(1952, 1973, 1981, 1986, 1995, 2004, 2007, 2013, 2020)] #>    Location  Time  PopTotal #>      <char> <num>     <num> #> 1:  Belgium  1952  8695.582 #> 2:  Belgium  1973  9727.980 #> 3:  Belgium  1981  9843.592 #> 4:  Belgium  1986  9887.815 #> 5:  Belgium  1995 10095.198 #> 6:  Belgium  2004 10456.163 #> 7:  Belgium  2007 10653.697 #> 8:  Belgium  2013 11103.257 #> 9:  Belgium  2020 11561.717 # rename columns setnames(wpp_data, c("Location", "Time"), c("country", "year")) # population in millions wpp_data[, PopTotal := PopTotal / 1000]  # coarsen to year - required as we don't have daily population data eu_membership <- as.data.table(socialchange::eu_membership) eu_membership[, year := year(as.Date(date))] ev <- decompose_events(eu_membership, wpp_data, PopTotal ~ country + year,   end_period = 2021, fun = sum) print(ev) #>     event_type      term    total      pct #>         <char>     <num>    <num>    <num> #> 1:      change  80.74284 265.3265 30.43151 #> 2: replacement 184.58363 265.3265 69.56849 plot(ev)
